title: Classification of goods and services
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS — private, public, merit, and demerit goods.
learningObjectives: Classify goods by rivalry and excludability.
Explain why markets fail for each type.

simpleExplanation.title: Not all goods are the same
simpleExplanation.summary: Economists classify goods to explain why free markets may over- or under-provide certain products.

flashcards.front: What makes a good a private good?
flashcards.back: Rival (one person's use reduces availability) and excludable (non-payers can be excluded) — e.g. food, clothing.

flashcards.front: What makes a good a public good?
flashcards.back: Non-rival and non-excludable — one person's use does not reduce others' use; free-rider problem prevents market supply.
A good underconsumed because consumers undervalue long-term benefits — e.g. education, vaccinations; positive externalities.
A good overconsumed because consumers ignore harm — e.g. tobacco, sugary drinks; negative externalities.

flashcards.front: Why do markets underprovide public goods?
flashcards.back: Free-rider problem — individuals benefit without paying, so firms cannot profitably supply them.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a quasi-public good.
flashcards.back: Roads or parks — may be non-rival at low use but become rival when congested; often partly excludable via tolls.

sections.content: **Classification of goods** (1.6) leads into **3.1 market failure**. Use a **2×2 grid** for public/private goods.

sections.question:
Classify each good and explain why the market may fail:

(a) National defence
(b) A cinema ticket
(c) Flu vaccination
(d) Cigarettes
sections.solution:
(a) **Public good** — non-rival and non-excludable. Free-rider problem → market will not supply; government provides.

(b) **Private good** — rival and excludable. Market works well; price rations access.

(c) **Merit good** — rival and excludable but **underconsumed** because individuals undervalue health benefits to society (positive externality). Market underprovides → subsidies or free provision (→ 3.2).

(d) **Demerit good** — rival and excludable but **overconsumed** because smokers ignore health costs to others and themselves (negative externality). Market overproduces → taxation or regulation (→ 3.2).
